steps.title: How fast you move energy
steps.body:
Two movers carry the same stack of boxes up the same stairs. One takes 1 minute, the other takes 10. They did the **same work** — but the fast one was far more **powerful**.

**Power** is the rate at which energy is transferred.

steps.title: The formula
steps.body: **W** is the work done (J), **Δt** is the time it took (s). Power is measured in **watts (W)**, where 1 watt = 1 joule per second.

steps.title: Get a feel for it
steps.body: Set the work and the time. Watch power shoot up as you shrink the time — same job, done faster, means more power.

steps.prompt: You do the same amount of work, but in half the time. What happens to your power output?
hints: P = W / Δt. Same work on top, smaller time on the bottom.
Half the time → double the power.

steps.prompt: Plug in the numbers. You do **W = 120 J** of work in **Δt = 4 s**. Drag each value into its spot, then find the power.
steps.fillHint: Work goes on top (W spot), time on the bottom (t spot). Here W = 120 and Δt = 4.

steps.title: Lifting at steady speed
steps.body: When a motor lifts a load at **constant speed**, there's no change in KE — all the work goes into gravitational PE. So the work done is just **W = mgh**, and power is that divided by time.

steps.prompt: A motor lifts a **4 kg** crate **2.5 m** at **constant speed** in **5 s**. Drag the motor's **P**.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You connected work, time, and power — the rate energy flows.
